Description: The Tokio runtime drives async tasks; use #[tokio::main] for applications or Runtime::new() for libraries
Do: Use #[tokio::main] with multi_thread flavor for servers; use current_thread for simple tools; configure worker_threads as needed
Dont: Block the runtime with synchronous code or create multiple runtimes unnecessarily

Description: Spawn concurrent async tasks that run independently on the Tokio runtime
Do: Use tokio::spawn for independent work; .await the JoinHandle when you need the result; ensure spawned futures are Send + 'static
Dont: Spawn tasks and ignore their JoinHandle losing errors; hold non-Send types across .await points

Keywords: mpsc, channel, sender, receiver, message passing
Description: Use tokio::sync::mpsc for multi-producer single-consumer message passing between async tasks
Do: Size the channel buffer appropriately; close senders to signal completion; handle RecvError to detect channel closure
Dont: Use unbounded channels without considering backpressure; ignore send errors
Code Bad: let (tx, mut rx) = mpsc::unbounded_channel::<String>();\n// no backpressure: producer can flood memory\n// in high-throughput systems this causes OOM

Description: Use tokio::sync::oneshot for sending a single value from one task to another
Do: Use oneshot for request-response patterns; pair with mpsc for command queues that expect replies
Dont: Use mpsc when only one value will ever be sent; keep the receiver alive after receiving

Description: Use tokio::sync::broadcast for one-to-many message distribution where each consumer gets every message
Do: Size the buffer for expected throughput; handle RecvError::Lagged when slow consumers fall behind; clone the sender for new producers
Dont: Use multiple mpsc channels to simulate broadcast; ignore the Lagged error

Keywords: watch, latest value, config reload, state
Description: Use tokio::sync::watch for sharing the latest value where receivers only care about the most recent state
Do: Use watch for configuration that changes infrequently; for sharing state snapshots; receivers call .changed().await to wait for updates
Dont: Use watch for high-throughput data streaming where every value matters (use mpsc instead)
Code Bad: let (tx, mut rx) = mpsc::channel(1);\n// for config: intermediate values are wasted\n// receiver only needs the latest config, not a queue

Description: Choose between Mutex (exclusive access) and RwLock (concurrent reads, exclusive writes) based on read/write ratio
Do: Use tokio::sync::Mutex for async code; use std::sync::Mutex for synchronous short critical sections; use RwLock when reads vastly outnumber writes
Dont: Hold a MutexGuard across .await points with std::sync::Mutex (will deadlock); use tokio::sync::Mutex if guard must be held across .await

Description: Use tokio::select! to race multiple async operations and act on the first one to complete
Do: Use select! for timeouts, graceful shutdown, and competing operations; pin futures that are polled across iterations
Dont: Forget that unselected branches are cancelled; use select! in a loop without pinning futures (creates new futures each iteration)

Description: Listen for OS signals and propagate shutdown through cancellation tokens or channels
Do: Use tokio_util::sync::CancellationToken or tokio::signal for clean shutdown; drain in-flight work before exiting
Dont: Call std::process::exit() directly; ignore SIGTERM in containerized environments

Description: Use tokio::sync::Semaphore to limit the number of concurrent operations
Do: Size the semaphore based on available resources; acquire permits before starting work; use forget() sparingly
Dont: Spawn unbounded tasks without limiting concurrency; forget to release permits

Description: Use tokio::task::JoinSet to manage a dynamic set of spawned tasks and collect their results
Do: Use JoinSet when spawning a variable number of tasks; call .join_next().await to collect results as they complete
Dont: Use Vec<JoinHandle> when JoinSet provides cleaner task management and automatic cleanup

Description: Run synchronous blocking code on a dedicated thread pool without blocking async workers
Do: Use tokio::task::spawn_blocking for CPU-heavy or blocking I/O that cannot be made async
Dont: Call blocking operations directly in async context; this blocks the Tokio worker thread

Keywords: rayon, par_iter, parallel, CPU bound, data parallel
Description: Use rayon parallel iterators for CPU-bound data processing across multiple cores
Do: Use par_iter() as a drop-in replacement for iter() on CPU-bound work; use par_sort for parallel sorting
Dont: Use rayon for I/O-bound work; mix rayon with Tokio without spawn_blocking

Description: Prevent deadlocks by acquiring locks in a consistent global order
Do: Always acquire multiple locks in the same order; use try_lock for timeout-based acquisition; minimize lock scope
Dont: Acquire locks in different orders across different code paths; hold locks while calling unknown code

Description: Use async streams to produce a sequence of values asynchronously, similar to async iterators
Do: Use the async-stream crate or implement Stream manually; use StreamExt for combinators like .next() and .map()
Dont: Collect all values into a Vec when lazy streaming would save memory and reduce latency

Description: Encapsulate mutable state in a task that processes messages sequentially via a channel
Do: Spawn a task with an mpsc receiver loop; expose a handle struct with async methods that send messages
Dont: Share mutable state with Arc<Mutex<T>> across many tasks when actor pattern is cleaner

Description: Use std::thread::scope to spawn threads that can borrow from the enclosing stack frame
Do: Use thread::scope when spawned threads need to reference local data; threads are guaranteed to join before scope exits
Dont: Spawn threads with std::thread::spawn and clone data unnecessarily; use Arc when scope would suffice

Description: Use atomic types for simple counters and flags without mutex overhead
Do: Use AtomicU64/AtomicBool for counters and flags; choose appropriate Ordering (Relaxed for counters, SeqCst when unsure)
Dont: Use Mutex for a single counter; use Relaxed ordering when you need happens-before guarantees

Description: Use CancellationToken from tokio-util for cooperative cancellation across task hierarchies
Do: Create child tokens from parent tokens; check cancellation in loops; use select! with token.cancelled()
Dont: Use abrupt task abort which may leave resources in inconsistent state

Description: Process many futures concurrently with bounded parallelism using StreamExt::buffer_unordered
Do: Use .buffer_unordered(n) to limit concurrency; use FuturesUnordered for manual control
Dont: Await futures sequentially when they are independent; spawn unbounded tasks

Description: Choose the right mutex type based on whether the lock is held across .await points
Do: Use std::sync::Mutex for short synchronous critical sections even in async code; use tokio::sync::Mutex only when guard must be held across .await
Dont: Always use tokio::sync::Mutex in async code (it has higher overhead); use std::sync::Mutex across .await points

Description: Use DashMap for a concurrent hash map with fine-grained locking through sharding
Do: Use DashMap for shared caches accessed by many tasks; use entry API for atomic insert-or-update; avoid holding refs across .await
Dont: Use Arc<Mutex<HashMap>> when DashMap provides better concurrency; hold DashMap refs across .await points

Description: Use a Barrier to synchronize multiple tasks/threads at a common point before proceeding
Do: Use Barrier when all participants must reach a checkpoint before any can proceed; for phased parallel algorithms
Dont: Use Barrier when tasks can proceed independently; when the number of participants is dynamic

Description: Use Notify to signal one or all waiting tasks to wake up without sending data
Do: Use Notify for simple wake-up signals; use notify_one() for single-consumer or notify_waiters() for broadcast
Dont: Use a channel just to send an empty message for signaling; over-engineer simple wake-up patterns

Description: Work around the lack of async Drop by explicitly calling cleanup methods or using a shutdown task
Do: Provide an explicit async shutdown/close method; use a background task for async cleanup; use AbortOnDrop wrappers
Dont: Assume Drop will run async cleanup (it cannot); block in Drop with block_on (panics inside Tokio)
